analysis.core_story_summary: Pashupat Vrata has been mentioned even in the Atharvasheersha Upanishad. It begins on the full moon day, in the month of Chaitra. It can be performed anywhere - a Shiva temple, any sacred place of pilgrimage, forest or garden.

audience_notes.children_8_12: Use simple language, explain divine names as characters in a sacred legend.
audience_notes.teens_13_17: Connect events to decision-making, identity, and consequences.
audience_notes.young_adults_18_30: Highlight ethics, leadership, and social relevance.
audience_notes.devotional_traditional: Preserve sacred framing and theological terms respectfully.
audience_notes.global_general: Keep cultural context brief and avoid insider assumptions.

sensitivity_notes: Contains conflict imagery that may need age-appropriate framing.

redesigned_story_by_demographic.children_8_12: **Pashupat Vrata and its Rituals — Kids (8-12):** Pashupat Vrata has been mentioned even in the Atharvasheersha Upanishad. It begins on the full moon day, in the month of Chaitra. It can be performed anywhere - a Shiva temple, any sacred place of pilgrimage, forest or garden. This story teaches the cost of conflict. Think of it like a legend that helps us choose kindness, courage, and respect.
redesigned_story_by_demographic.teens_13_17: **Pashupat Vrata and its Rituals — Teens (13-17):** Pashupat Vrata has been mentioned even in the Atharvasheersha Upanishad. It begins on the full moon day, in the month of Chaitra. It can be performed anywhere - a Shiva temple, any sacred place of pilgrimage, forest or garden. For teens, the key idea is the cost of conflict. It shows how choices, discipline, and character shape outcomes.
redesigned_story_by_demographic.young_adults_18_30: **Pashupat Vrata and its Rituals — Young Adults:** Pashupat Vrata has been mentioned even in the Atharvasheersha Upanishad. It begins on the full moon day, in the month of Chaitra. It can be performed anywhere - a Shiva temple, any sacred place of pilgrimage, forest or garden. A modern takeaway is the cost of conflict. The narrative can be read as a reflection on purpose, responsibility, and values-driven action.
redesigned_story_by_demographic.devotional_traditional: **Pashupat Vrata and its Rituals — Devotional/Traditional Readers:** Pashupat Vrata has been mentioned even in the Atharvasheersha Upanishad. It begins on the full moon day, in the month of Chaitra. It can be performed anywhere - a Shiva temple, any sacred place of pilgrimage, forest or garden. The episode highlights the cost of conflict. It preserves reverence for divine agency while encouraging faithful practice and humility.
redesigned_story_by_demographic.global_general: **Pashupat Vrata and its Rituals — Global General Audience:** Pashupat Vrata has been mentioned even in the Atharvasheersha Upanishad. It begins on the full moon day, in the month of Chaitra. It can be performed anywhere - a Shiva temple, any sacred place of pilgrimage, forest or garden. In universal terms, it explores the cost of conflict. Cultural names are preserved while the message is presented in accessible contemporary English.
